passages.explanation: Jesus, his mother, and his disciples attend a wedding in Cana. When the wine runs out — a serious social embarrassment for the host — Jesus' mother tells him about it, clearly expecting him to do something. Jesus pushes back, saying his time hasn't come yet. But his mother ignores the pushback and tells the servants to do whatever he says. She's forcing his hand, and she clearly knows he has the ability to act.

Jesus tells the servants to fill six large stone water jars and then draw some out for the master of ceremonies. The water has become wine — and not cheap wine, but the best at the entire feast. The master of ceremonies is confused because hosts normally serve the good wine first and save the cheap stuff for when everyone is too drunk to notice. John calls this Jesus' first 'sign' — not just a miracle for its own sake, but an event that reveals who Jesus is. His disciples see it and believe in him.

A brief transition. Jesus moves to Capernaum with his family and disciples and stays a few days. This sets up the shift from a small-town celebration to the major confrontation that follows in Jerusalem.

Jesus arrives in Jerusalem for Passover and finds the temple courtyard full of merchants selling animals for sacrifice and exchanging currency — both legitimate services, but they had turned God's house into a commercial operation. Jesus is furious. He makes a whip, drives out the animals, flips the money tables, and orders the dove sellers out. This isn't gentle Jesus — this is aggressive, physical, and deliberate. His disciples connect his behaviour to a scripture about being consumed by passion for God's house.

The Jewish authorities demand Jesus prove he has the right to do what he just did. Jesus responds with a riddle: 'Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up.' They think he's talking about the building, which took 46 years to construct. He's actually talking about his own body — predicting his death and resurrection. John notes that the disciples only understood this after Jesus rose from the dead. This is a pattern in John's gospel: Jesus says things with a deeper meaning that people around him consistently miss.

Many people in Jerusalem believe in Jesus after seeing his miracles during the festival. But Jesus doesn't trust them back. He knows that belief based only on seeing impressive signs is shallow and unreliable. He doesn't need anyone to tell him what people are really like — he already knows. This is a warning from John: being impressed by Jesus is not the same as genuinely following him.
